hero.title: Primitives Overview
hero.description: This section provides a reference for all shared UI primitives. Browse the categories below to explore actionable, display, form, navigation, overlay, and structure components.

organization.title: Organized by role
organization.description: Primitives are grouped by their primary UI role for clarity and discoverability.

patterns.title: Consistent patterns
patterns.description: All primitives follow the same design system and interaction patterns.

exploration.title: Explore by category
exploration.description: Use the cards below to jump into each primitive category and see detailed docs.

categories.title: Primitive Categories
categories.description: Each category contains related primitives. Click a card to view all primitives in that group.

actions.label: Actions
actions.title: Actions
actions.description: Buttons, toggles, and other actionable primitives.

display.label: Display
display.title: Display
display.description: Cards, badges, icons, and visual display elements.

forms.label: Forms
forms.title: Forms
forms.description: Inputs, selects, checkboxes, and form controls.

navigation.label: Navigation
navigation.title: Navigation
navigation.description: Tabs, accordions, breadcrumbs, and navigation aids.

overlays.label: Overlays
overlays.title: Overlays
overlays.description: Dialogs, popovers, tooltips, and overlay components.

structure.label: Structure
structure.title: Structure
structure.description: Layout, scroll areas, and structural primitives.

skeleton.title: Skeleton
skeleton.description: Skeleton is the loading primitive for content that already has a stable layout. In most cases, use it through `Card isLoading` so card slots swap to placeholders automatically.

items.automatic: Prefer automatic loading states for cards. Set `isLoading` on the card and keep the real card structure in place.
items.scope: Use skeletons in the same container that will eventually render the real content so spacing and hierarchy stay stable.
items.stability: Loading placeholders should preserve layout footprint. Avoid swapping between completely different structures while requests are in flight.
items.manual: Use the low-level `Skeleton` primitive directly only when you need custom placeholder shapes outside the built-in card anatomy.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Card
  isLoading
  className="flex min-h-40 flex-col justify-between p-4"
>
  <CardHeader>
    <CardTitle>Card title</CardTitle>
    <CardDescription>
      Supporting description.
    </CardDescription>
  </CardHeader>

  <CardContent>
    <Button usage="primary">Continue</Button>
  </CardContent>
</Card>

<Skeleton className="h-4 w-24" />

cards.title: Automatic card loading
cards.description: The card primitive can now replace title, description, content, and footer slots with skeleton placeholders automatically.

buttonUsage.title: Applied to existing card JSX
buttonUsage.description: This is the same card structure used in the button docs, but with `isLoading` enabled on the card.

section.title: Section loading
section.description: Sections can also switch their title, description, actions, and content to loading placeholders through the same pattern.

dialog.title: Dialog loading
dialog.description: Dialogs use the same loading context so their title, description, body region, and footer actions stay structurally stable.

icon.title: Icon loading
icon.description: Standalone icons can expose a simple circular skeleton when the icon itself is still resolving.

layout.title: Layout loading
layout.description: Low-level `Header`, `Content`, and `Footer` primitives also support explicit loading placeholders for custom compositions.

primitive.title: Low-level primitive
primitive.description: Use the `Skeleton` primitive directly for layouts that do not map to the built-in card anatomy.

cardExample.title: Project summary
cardExample.description: This card keeps the exact same JSX in both states. Only the `isLoading` prop changes.

sectionExample.title: Workspace health
sectionExample.description: Use the same section structure during loading so the page rhythm stays stable.
sectionExample.content: The section body can keep its final layout while the content resolves.

dialogExample.title: Sync workspace
dialogExample.description: The dialog keeps its structure while waiting for the content of the current step.
dialogExample.content: Once loaded, the body content replaces the placeholder without changing the overall dialog anatomy.
dialogExample.note: Open the preview to inspect the dialog loading state in place.

skeleton.primitiveNote: Use the low-level primitive only when automatic card loading is not enough.

popover.title: Popover
popover.description: Popovers reveal compact, contextual content anchored to a trigger. Use them for inline filters, supporting actions, and lightweight panels that should stay connected to the current layout.

items.inline: Use popovers for content that belongs to the current surface and does not need the interruption level of a dialog.
items.scope: Keep the content compact and tightly scoped to the trigger. If the content becomes long or multi-step, escalate to a dialog or a dedicated route.
items.content: Popover content can mix text, metadata, small forms, and action groups, but it should still feel like one short contextual panel.
items.escalation: Use a popover when users should stay in the current flow. Use a tooltip for hints, a dropdown menu for short action lists, and a dialog for focused tasks.

patternNotes.snippet:
Refine results

Short contextual panel.

filters.title: Inline filters
filters.description: A popover works well when filters belong to the current list or card and should stay attached to the trigger.

actions.title: Actions and supporting panels
actions.description: Use the same primitive for richer action lists and lightweight contextual panels.

filters.title: Refine results
filters.description: Keep lightweight filters close to the data set they affect.

actions.title: Context actions
actions.description: A popover can hold denser actions when a plain tooltip is too small and a dialog would be too disruptive.

inline.title: Lightweight panel
inline.description: Use the primitive for compact contextual content that should stay anchored near the interaction point.
inline.panelDescription: This kind of content stays close to the trigger instead of interrupting the screen.

dropdownMenu.title: Dropdown Menu
dropdownMenu.description: Dropdown menus expose compact action lists anchored to a trigger. Use them for row actions, card utilities, and grouped secondary commands that would add too much noise as inline buttons.

items.compact: Use dropdown menus for short command lists that belong to one trigger and do not need rich supporting layout.
items.scope: Keep the menu tightly scoped to actions for the current object, row, or surface. If the content becomes explanatory or form-like, switch to a popover or dialog.
items.grouping: Group related actions with labels and separators when the menu mixes primary utilities with lower-frequency or riskier actions.
items.escalation: Use a dropdown menu for compact action selection, a popover for richer lightweight panels, and an alert dialog for consequential confirmation.

patternNotes.snippet:
<DropdownMenu>
  <DropdownMenuTrigger>
    <Button size="icon" usage="secondary" variant="ghost">...</Button>
  </DropdownMenuTrigger>

  <DropdownMenuContent>
    <DropdownMenuLabel>Actions</DropdownMenuLabel>
    <DropdownMenuItem>View details</DropdownMenuItem>
    <DropdownMenuSeparator />
    <DropdownMenuItem>Archive</DropdownMenuItem>
  </DropdownMenuContent>
</DropdownMenu>

actions.title: Compact actions
actions.description: Dropdown menus work best when several supporting actions need to stay available without taking over the layout.

rowActions.title: Row action menu
rowActions.description: A common admin pattern for compact table or card-row actions.

grouped.title: Grouped utilities
grouped.description: Use labels and separators when the menu needs a little more structure without becoming a larger panel.

select.title: Select
select.description: Select lets users choose one option from a compact list. Use it for filters, form fields, and scoped configuration choices where the current value should remain visible in a single trigger field.

items.scope: Use select when the user must choose one value from a defined set and the currently selected option should stay visible in the field.
items.placeholder: Use a placeholder only when no default selection should be implied. Once a choice exists, the trigger should show the selected value directly.
items.grouping: Group options with labels and separators when the list mixes categories or lifecycle stages that benefit from lightweight structure.
items.forms: Select works well for forms and filters, but if the content needs searching, multi-selection, or richer metadata, consider a different pattern.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Select value={value} onValueChange={setValue}>
  <SelectTrigger placeholder="Choose status" />
  <SelectContent>
    <SelectItem value="active">Active</SelectItem>
    <SelectItem value="paused">Paused</SelectItem>
  </SelectContent>
</Select>

filters.title: Filter and field selection
filters.description: Select is a strong fit for compact filters and form fields that expose one current value at a time.

groups.title: Grouped options
groups.description: Use labels and separators when a flat list would be harder to scan or reason about.

status.title: Controlled status filter
status.description: A typical admin filter where the selected value is controlled by surrounding state.
status.label: Status

placeholder.title: Unselected field
placeholder.description: Use a placeholder when the user still needs to make an explicit first selection.
placeholder.label: Date range

grouped.title: Grouped workspace actions
grouped.description: A grouped list can stay compact while still exposing lightweight hierarchy inside the option set.
grouped.label: Workspace target

selectionControls.title: Selection Controls
selectionControls.description: Checkbox, radio group, and switch all represent user choice, but they communicate different interaction models. Use them according to whether the user is selecting many items, exactly one option, or toggling a setting on and off.

items.checkbox: Use checkbox when the user can independently select zero, one, or many options from a set.
items.radio: Use radio group when exactly one option must be chosen from several mutually exclusive choices.
items.switch: Use switch for immediate on/off settings and binary preferences that read naturally as enabled or disabled.
items.scope: Do not swap these controls casually. The chosen control should match the meaning of the decision, not just the amount of available space.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Checkbox label="Share progress updates" />

<RadioGroup value={value} onValueChange={setValue}>
  <RadioGroupItem value="email" label="Email" />
  <RadioGroupItem value="sms" label="SMS" />
</RadioGroup>

<Switch label="Enable notifications" />

comparison.title: Interaction comparison
comparison.description: These three controls belong together conceptually, but each one signals a different kind of user decision.

checkbox.title: Checkbox
checkbox.description: Independent choices where more than one option may be enabled at the same time.

disabled.label: Locked setting
disabled.description: This option is unavailable in the current workflow.

one.label: Share weekly summary
one.description: Send a compact digest of project updates every Friday.

two.label: Include archived workspaces
two.description: Keep archived items in the same reporting scope.

radio.title: Radio group
radio.description: Exclusive choices where one communication mode or policy must be selected.

email.label: Email
email.description: Send detailed updates to the primary inbox.

sms.label: SMS
sms.description: Use a shorter message for urgent contact only.

silent.label: Silent
silent.description: Store the update without sending an active notification.

switch.title: Switch
switch.description: A binary setting that reads clearly as on or off in the current state.

disabled.label: System lock
disabled.description: This preference is controlled by workspace policy.

item.label: Enable notifications
item.description: Immediately allow alerts for overdue tasks and project status changes.

toggleControls.title: Toggle Controls
toggleControls.description: Toggle and toggle group express pressed state rather than form selection. Use them for view modes, formatting controls, and dense action sets where the control should behave like a button with remembered state.

items.toggle: Use toggle for one pressed/unpressed control that behaves like a stateful action button.
items.group: Use toggle group when several related pressed controls need to behave as one mutually exclusive or multi-select set.
items.press: These controls communicate pressed state, not form semantics. Prefer checkbox, radio group, or switch when the meaning is selection or settings.
items.scope: Toggle controls are strongest in toolbars, view switches, formatting actions, and compact filter chips.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Toggle aria-label="Bold">Bold</Toggle>

<ToggleGroup value={value} onValueChange={setValue}>
  <ToggleGroupItem value="grid">Grid</ToggleGroupItem>
  <ToggleGroupItem value="list">List</ToggleGroupItem>
</ToggleGroup>

usage.title: Single and exclusive usage
usage.description: Use a lone toggle for one pressed action, or a single-value toggle group when one view or mode should stay active.

multi.title: Multi-pressed groups
multi.description: A multiple toggle group works when several formatting or filter states may remain active together.

single.title: Single toggle
single.description: A pressed action that behaves like a stateful button instead of a form field.
single.label: Bold

view.title: Exclusive view mode
view.description: A single-value toggle group is a good fit for compact view switches such as grid versus list.

multi.title: Multiple pressed states
multi.description: Use multi-select toggle groups for formatting or chip-like controls where several states may remain active.

structurePrimitives.title: Structure Primitives
structurePrimitives.description: Tabs, label, separator, and scroll area shape layout and interaction rhythm rather than carrying heavy business meaning on their own. Use them to organize content, connect fields to text, and manage dense surfaces with clearer structure.

items.tabs: Use tabs when several peer sections share one space and users should switch between them without leaving the current page context.
items.label: Use label to associate form text with its field directly instead of relying on nearby typography alone.
items.separator: Use separators sparingly to reinforce grouping or rhythm. They should support hierarchy, not replace spacing and layout decisions.
items.scrollArea: Use scroll areas for constrained regions such as sidebars, panels, and dense lists where native overflow needs better visual handling.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Tabs defaultValue="overview">
  <TabsList>
    <TabsTrigger value="overview">Overview</TabsTrigger>
    <TabsTrigger value="members">Members</TabsTrigger>
  </TabsList>

  <TabsContent value="overview">...</TabsContent>
</Tabs>

<Label htmlFor="workspace-name">Workspace name</Label>
<Separator />
<ScrollArea className="h-48">...</ScrollArea>

tabs.title: Tabs
tabs.description: Tabs are a strong fit when related sections compete for the same page space but should remain quickly accessible.

structure.title: Label, separator, and scroll area
structure.description: These primitives become most useful when they reinforce form structure, metadata rhythm, and constrained overflow in the same composition.

overview.label: Overview
overview.title: Workspace overview
overview.description: Use a tab when this panel is one peer among several related content areas.

members.label: Members
members.title: Members and roles
members.description: Tabs keep related sections adjacent without forcing every detail into one long surface.

history.label: History
history.title: Activity history
history.description: This is useful when users need quick context switching between parallel views.

labelCard.title: Field structure
labelCard.description: Labels and separators support small but important hierarchy decisions inside forms and metadata rows.

scrollCard.title: Constrained scrolling
scrollCard.description: Scroll areas work well inside panels or cards where longer lists should remain bounded without losing polish.

one.title: Activity item one
one.description: Scrollable regions should still preserve readable rhythm and spacing.

two.title: Activity item two
two.description: Use a scroll area when the parent surface should stay bounded.

three.title: Activity item three
three.description: This is especially useful for side panels and compact dashboards.

four.title: Activity item four
four.description: The primitive keeps overflow behavior explicit inside the region.

five.title: Activity item five
five.description: Longer collections should remain easy to scan even when constrained.

six.title: Activity item six
six.description: The surrounding card can stay visually stable while the list scrolls.

input.title: Input
input.description: Input is the base text field primitive for short single-line values. Use it for identifiers, email, passwords, search, and compact form fields where the value should stay visible on one line.

items.base: Use input for short-form values that fit naturally on one line and should remain directly editable in place.
items.password: Password fields can expose a reveal toggle, but the primitive should still behave like one field rather than a separate custom pattern.
items.icons: Leading icons are useful for lightweight context such as search or email, but they should remain supportive rather than carrying essential meaning alone.
items.scope: If the content becomes long-form or multi-paragraph, switch to a text area instead of stretching the input pattern.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Input type="email" placeholder="admin@pug.edu.br" />

<Input type="password" showPasswordToggle />

fields.title: Common fields
fields.description: The same primitive should handle standard text entry and password reveal behavior without fragmenting the API.

search.title: Search field
search.description: A lightweight icon can help the field read as search without changing the underlying primitive.

email.title: Email field
email.description: A standard single-line field with lightweight leading context.
email.label: Email

password.title: Password field
password.description: Use the built-in reveal behavior when the field should let users inspect the hidden value temporarily.
password.label: Password

disabled.title: Disabled field
disabled.description: Inputs should clearly show when they are unavailable.
disabled.label: Workspace name

textArea.title: Text Area
textArea.description: Text area is the primitive for longer freeform content. Use it when the value needs more than one line, such as notes, feedback, descriptions, or explanatory content.

items.longform: Use text area when the user is expected to write sentences, notes, or any content that naturally spans multiple lines.
items.resize: The field should preserve a comfortable minimum height so longer content does not feel cramped from the first interaction.
items.scope: Do not use a text area for short structured values just because more width is available. Keep single-line fields as inputs.
items.rhythm: Labels, spacing, and surrounding form rhythm matter more with text areas because they occupy noticeably more vertical space.

patternNotes.snippet: <TextArea placeholder="Add internal notes" />

usage.title: Long-form entry
usage.description: Text areas are best for content that should stay readable while it grows vertically.

notes.title: Internal notes
notes.description: A compact long-form field for operational notes or supporting detail.
notes.label: Notes

feedback.title: Longer feedback
feedback.description: Use a taller text area when the expected response is more descriptive and reflective.
feedback.label: Feedback
feedback.placeholder: Describe the current experience and what should improve

datePicker.title: Date Picker
datePicker.description: Date picker captures one calendar date and time in a compact field. This implementation uses a styled calendar popover so the control fits the design system while still staying lightweight and focused on single-value scheduling.

items.native: Use this primitive when the product needs one scheduled point in time and the field should still look and behave like the rest of the form system.
items.scope: This primitive is best for one-off date and time fields such as meetings, due timestamps, releases, and reporting cutoffs.
items.constraints: Use `min` and `max` when the valid scheduling window is known so both the calendar and time selectors communicate the allowed bounds.
items.escalation: If the product later needs ranges, presets, recurring rules, or complex timeline logic, that should become a higher-level composite component instead of stretching this baseline primitive.

patternNotes.snippet:
<DatePicker defaultValue="2026-04-24T14:30" />

<DatePicker min="2026-04-20T09:00" max="2026-04-30T18:00" />

fields.title: Date and time fields
fields.description: Use the picker when the user needs one timestamp and the control should stay aligned with the shared styling system.

basic.title: Basic date-time field
basic.description: Use it when the user needs one scheduled moment with direct calendar and time selection.
basic.label: Review meeting

constrained.title: Constrained window
constrained.description: Min and max boundaries help communicate valid booking windows directly in the picker.
constrained.label: Publishing window

disabled.title: Disabled schedule
disabled.description: Use a disabled date picker when the scheduled value is fixed by the surrounding workflow.
disabled.label: Enrollment start

multiSelect.title: Multi Select
multiSelect.description: Multi select lets users choose several values from one compact field. Use it when multiple filters or assignments belong to the same category and should stay grouped under one trigger.

items.scope: Use multi select when the user may choose several values from the same set without needing separate checkboxes in the main layout.
items.summary: The trigger should summarize the current selection compactly instead of trying to render a long unchecked list in the closed state.
items.options: Descriptions can help when option labels alone are not enough, but the list should still remain easy to scan and toggle quickly.
items.escalation: If the option set becomes very large, searchable, or deeply grouped, this should evolve into a richer composite pattern instead of stretching the simple multi-select too far.

patternNotes.snippet:
<MultiSelect
  value={value}
  onValueChange={setValue}
  placeholder="Choose statuses"
  options={[
    { value: "active", label: "Active" },
    { value: "paused", label: "Paused" },
  ]}
/>

filters.title: Grouped multi-value fields
filters.description: Multi select is a good fit when several filters or assignments should stay available in one compact field.

status.title: Status filters
status.description: A common admin filter case where several statuses may remain active together.
status.label: Statuses

owners.title: Owners and assignees
owners.description: Descriptions can help when the selected values represent people or roles rather than simple tags.
owners.label: Owners

accordion.title: Accordion
accordion.description: Accordions reveal and hide related sections of content within the current page flow. Use them when information should stay available on the page without forcing every detail to be visible at once.

items.disclosure: Use accordions for progressive disclosure when each section can be scanned by its trigger label before the body is opened.
items.trigger: Trigger labels should be short and descriptive enough for users to decide whether the section is relevant before expanding it.
items.content: Accordion content can hold text, metadata, controls, or nested layout blocks, but each item should still represent one coherent topic.
items.multi: Use `type="single"` when sections should compete for attention, and `type="multiple"` when users may need to compare or keep several sections open.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Accordion type="single" collapsible>
  <AccordionItem value="overview">
    <AccordionTrigger>Overview</AccordionTrigger>
    <AccordionContent>
      Body content
    </AccordionContent>
  </AccordionItem>
</Accordion>

structure.title: Structure
structure.description: Accordion items should read clearly in both collapsed and expanded states.

compact.title: Compact and multi-open usage
compact.description: The same primitive works for compact FAQ-style content and denser settings groups.

overview.title: Project overview
overview.description: Keep the highest-level summary behind a short trigger when the page already has competing content around it.

roles.title: Roles and owners
roles.description: Use an accordion item when supporting ownership details should be reachable without permanently occupying the layout.

delivery.title: Delivery notes
delivery.description: Longer explanatory content can live inside the expanded panel as long as the trigger still represents a single topic.

compactCard.title: Compact disclosure
compactCard.description: A denser accordion still works if each item remains easy to scan and the labels stay explicit.

timing.title: Response timing
timing.description: Use the compact form for short operational guidance and support answers.

ownership.title: Ownership rules
ownership.description: The body should answer one narrow question instead of becoming a page hidden behind a trigger.

escalation.title: Escalation path
escalation.description: Keep the label decisive so users can tell what they will reveal before opening the item.

multiCard.title: Multiple open sections
multiCard.description: Allow multiple items to remain open when users may need to compare settings or keep several groups visible.

filters.title: Saved filters
filters.description: Useful when users need to inspect several configuration groups at the same time.

visibility.title: Visibility rules
visibility.description: Multi-open mode reduces unnecessary toggling when the user is reviewing related details.

history.title: Change history
history.description: Use it when independent sections may all deserve attention during the same task.

button.title: Buttons
button.description: Buttons trigger user actions. Use `usage` to define semantic intent, `variant` to define visual treatment, and `size` to adapt the component to layout density.

items.usage: Set `usage` according to the meaning of the action: primary, secondary, success, info, warning, or danger.
items.flat: Use `variant="flat"` for the default call-to-action treatment.
items.ghost: Use `variant="ghost"` for lower-emphasis actions, dense interfaces, and supporting controls.
items.icon: For icon-only buttons, always provide a readable `title`, `aria-label`, or `tooltipContent` so the component can expose an accessible name.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Button
  usage="primary | secondary | success | info | warning | danger"
  variant="flat | ghost"
  size="sm | md | lg | icon"
  leadingIcon={<Icon />}
  trailingIcon={<Icon />}
  isLoading
  loadingText="Saving..."
  tooltipContent="Add item"
/>

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Choose the semantic usage that matches the outcome of the action.

variant.title: Variant
variant.description: Variants change presentation without changing behavior or semantic intent.

scale.title: Scale
scale.description: Scale the component according to spacing and density requirements.

states.title: States
states.description: Use the built-in states to keep feedback and interaction rules consistent.

primary.name: Primary
primary.description: Default high-emphasis action for confirm and submit flows.
primary.label: Save changes

secondary.name: Secondary
secondary.description: Neutral action for companion paths and supporting controls.
secondary.label: Export report

success.name: Success
success.description: Positive action for approvals, completion, or confirmation.
success.label: Mark approved

info.name: Info
info.description: Guidance action when you need emphasis without implying risk.
info.label: View details

warning.name: Warning
warning.description: Cautionary action that deserves review before continuing.
warning.label: Review issue

danger.name: Danger
danger.description: Destructive action for irreversible changes and deletion.
danger.label: Revoke access

flat.name: Flat
flat.description: Filled in light mode and softened in dark mode without changing the action meaning.

ghost.name: Ghost
ghost.description: Low-chrome action for dense toolbars, lists, and utility affordances.

card.title: Card
card.description: Cards group related content into a bounded surface. Use them as layout containers for summaries, supporting actions, and content blocks that should read as a single unit.

items.container: Use cards to group related information, not to create arbitrary visual boxes around every element on the page.
items.structure: Compose cards from header, description, content, and footer only when those slots are actually needed by the content.
items.footer: Use the footer for card-level actions. If the card has no actions, omit the footer entirely.
items.behavior: Cards are structural primitives. Behavior such as dialogs, tooltips, dropdowns, and navigation should come from the elements placed inside them, not from the card itself.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Card>
  <CardHeader>
    <CardTitle>Card title</CardTitle>
    <CardDescription>
      Short supporting description.
    </CardDescription>
  </CardHeader>

  <CardContent>Body content</CardContent>

  <CardFooter>
    <Button variant="ghost">Secondary</Button>
    <Button usage="primary" variant="flat">Primary</Button>
  </CardFooter>
</Card>

structure.title: Structure
structure.description: Cards should expose a predictable internal anatomy without forcing every slot into every use case.

composition.title: Composition
composition.description: Use the same primitive for simple information blocks and for cards that include supporting actions.

layouts.title: Layout patterns
layouts.description: Cards can host denser layouts as long as the content still reads as one coherent block.

basic.title: Basic content block
basic.description: A card may be nothing more than a title, a short description, and one body region.
basic.body: Use this shape for documentation notes, summaries, and supporting content that should sit inside a clear boundary.

header.title: Header is optional
header.description: Use `CardHeader` when the content needs a visible title and supporting description before the body.
header.body: If the body already carries enough context, do not force extra header copy just to fill space.

actions.title: Card with actions
actions.description: Some cards need a footer because they collect card-level decisions or shortcuts.
actions.body: Keep footer actions tied to the content of that card. If actions belong to the entire page or section, place them outside the card instead.

summary.title: Summary card
summary.description: Cards can also hold compact dashboards and metadata without needing a footer.

list.title: Grouped list content
list.description: A card can contain multiple internal rows when they all belong to the same summary or task.

overview.title: Overview
overview.description: Use a shared surface when rows are part of the same conceptual block.

composition.title: Composition
composition.description: Keep spacing and internal hierarchy consistent across all rows.

actions.title: Actions stay scoped
actions.description: If a row needs an action, it should still feel like part of the same card.

compact.title: Compact marker
compact.description: Cards do not need to be tall. Compact content works when the boundary still improves scanability.

dialog.title: Dialog
dialog.description: Dialogs interrupt the current flow to present focused content, decisions, or structured tasks. Use them for content that needs explicit attention without leaving the current page.

items.useCase: Use dialogs for focused tasks, confirmations, or supporting content that should temporarily take priority over the page.
items.structure: Keep the structure predictable: header for title and description, scrollable body for content, and footer for actions.
items.actions: Use the footer for the action hierarchy required by the flow. Some dialogs need one action, some need two, and only some need the full primary/secondary/tertiary set.
items.overflow: When content grows, let only the body scroll. Header and footer should remain fixed within the dialog.

patternNotes.snippet:
Dialog title Short explanation for the dialog.
Body content

structure.title: Structure
structure.description: Use a consistent layout so users can immediately identify the dialog header, body, and actions.

footer.title: Footer actions
footer.description: Reserve the footer for the action hierarchy instead of mixing structural actions into the body.

overflow.title: Overflow
overflow.description: Long content should scroll inside the dialog body while header and footer remain fixed.

basic.title: Basic structure
basic.description: A minimal dialog should provide a clear title, a short description, focused content, and explicit dismissal paths.

description.title: Descriptive header
description.description: Use the description slot for one concise sentence that explains the purpose of the dialog before the body content starts.

footerCard.title: Action combinations
footerCard.description: Dialog footers do not need three actions every time. Use only the actions the task actually requires, and vary the button usage by the intent of the flow.

overflowCard.title: Scrollable body
overflowCard.description: When the dialog body is long, only the content region should scroll so context and actions remain visible.

basic.title: Create workspace
basic.description: Use a short description to explain what the dialog is for before the user reviews the body content.
basic.body: This example shows a common two-action dialog: a dismissive secondary action and a single primary action for completion.

footer.title: Delete project
footer.description: This example shows a destructive footer with a low-emphasis tertiary action, a secondary dismissal path, and a danger primary action.

overflow.title: Migration notes
overflow.description: The body region handles overflow so the title and footer remain visible while the user reads longer content.

paragraphs.one: Dialogs should still feel stable when content becomes long. Header information needs to stay visible so the user never loses context.
paragraphs.two: The body is the only region that should scroll. That keeps the action row anchored and prevents the footer from drifting out of view.
paragraphs.three: Long-form content in a dialog should still be focused. If the content starts to resemble a full page, reconsider whether a dedicated route is a better fit.
paragraphs.four: Descriptions belong near the title, not buried inside the content body. That keeps the purpose of the dialog clear before the user interacts with the details.
paragraphs.five: Footer actions should remain compact and predictable. Use the same action order consistently so the primary decision stays easy to find.
paragraphs.six: This example intentionally adds enough content to force overflow so the scrolling behavior can be evaluated in a realistic documentation scenario.

alertDialog.title: Alert Dialog
alertDialog.description: Alert dialogs interrupt the current flow for consequential confirmations. Use them when the user must acknowledge risk, permanence, or a meaningful state change before continuing.

items.severity: Use alert dialogs for destructive, high-impact, or irreversible actions that deserve a stronger confirmation pattern than a normal dialog.
items.confirmation: Keep the title and description explicit about what will happen, what is affected, and whether the action can be undone.
items.actions: The footer should stay simple: a clear cancel path and one confirm action with styling that matches the level of risk.
items.loading: Loading support is useful when the confirmation copy or decision context depends on fetched data, but it should remain a scoped exception rather than the default state.

patternNotes.snippet:
<AlertDialog open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
  <AlertDialogContent>
    <AlertDialogHeader>
      <AlertDialogTitle>Delete project</AlertDialogTitle>
      <AlertDialogDescription>
        This action cannot be undone.
      </AlertDialogDescription>
    </AlertDialogHeader>

    <AlertDialogFooter>
      <AlertDialogCancel>
        <Button usage="secondary" variant="ghost">Cancel</Button>
      </AlertDialogCancel>
      <AlertDialogAction>
        <Button usage="danger" variant="flat">Delete</Button>
      </AlertDialogAction>
    </AlertDialogFooter>
  </AlertDialogContent>
</AlertDialog>

confirmation.title: Confirmation patterns
confirmation.description: Use alert dialogs when the action deserves a forced confirmation with a narrow set of choices.

loading.title: Loading confirmation state
loading.description: If the final confirmation depends on fetched context, the same primitive can hold a temporary loading state without changing the overall anatomy.

delete.title: Destructive confirmation
delete.description: A strong alert dialog pattern for permanent removal or irreversible revocation.

archive.title: Cautionary confirmation
archive.description: Not every alert dialog is fully destructive, but it should still reflect meaningful impact and explicit intent.

loadingCard.title: Loading before confirm
loadingCard.description: Use loading only when the confirmation text depends on context that is still resolving.

delete.title: Delete project
delete.description: Deleting this project permanently removes its records and cannot be undone.

archive.title: Archive workspace
archive.description: Archiving removes the workspace from active views and pauses new activity until it is restored.

loadingExample.title: Delete project
loadingExample.description: The final confirmation details are still being loaded before the destructive action is shown.

emptyState.title: Empty State
emptyState.description: Empty states communicate that content is absent and explain what the user can do next. Use them for first-use moments, empty results, filtered views, and access-related absence of content.

items.context: Match the empty state to the reason content is missing: first use, no results, no access, or a temporarily empty dataset.
items.message: Lead with a clear title and short description that explains why nothing is shown without blaming the user.
items.actions: Add actions only when the user has a meaningful next step. Do not add buttons just to fill the space.
items.scope: Use empty states inside the container that owns the absence of content: a page section, a card, a table region, or a list body.

patternNotes.snippet:
<EmptyState
  icon={<Inbox />}
  title="No items yet"
  description="Create the first item to populate this view."
  actions={
    <Button usage="primary" variant="flat">New item</Button>
  }
/>

firstUse.title: First-use state
firstUse.description: Use a more inviting empty state when the user has not created any content yet.

results.title: Results and access states
results.description: Different absence cases need different tone and actions.

compact.title: Compact inline state
compact.description: Use a smaller version when the empty state lives inside a tighter region such as a card or side panel.

firstUse.title: No projects yet
firstUse.description: Create the first project to start organizing work, assigning owners, and tracking progress from this area.

noResults.title: No matching results
noResults.description: No records matched the current filters. Adjust the criteria or clear filters to broaden the results.

noAccess.title: No accessible workspaces
noAccess.description: This user is not assigned to any workspace yet. Grant access before expecting content in this area.

compact.title: Nothing to review
compact.description: This queue is empty right now.

icon.title: Icon
icon.description: Icons communicate supporting meaning, reinforce actions, and compact visual status. Use the icon primitive so decorative icons stay silent, labeled icons remain accessible, and standalone icons can expose tooltip help when needed.

items.decorative: Use icons as decorative by default when adjacent text already communicates the meaning.
items.accessible: Provide a `label` when the icon must expose meaning on its own. The primitive uses Radix Accessible Icon for that accessible name.
items.tooltip: A labeled standalone icon can also expose a tooltip. If no custom tooltip is provided, the label is reused as the hint.
items.buttons: Inside buttons and other controls, keep the inner icon decorative. Accessibility and tooltip behavior should usually belong to the parent control.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Icon
  icon={Search}
  label="Search"
  tooltipContent="Find records"
  size={16}
  className="text-[color:var(--color-brand)]"
/>

decorative.title: Decorative usage
decorative.description: Most icons are supporting visuals and should not create redundant accessibility output.

accessibility.title: Accessibility and tooltip
accessibility.description: When an icon stands alone, give it an accessible label and optionally a custom tooltip.

integration.title: Integration
integration.description: Use the same primitive for standalone icons and icons composed inside buttons or other controls.

inline.title: Inline support icon
inline.description: Decorative icons should reinforce nearby text without competing with it in the accessibility tree.

status.title: Status badge
status.description: Compact status markers often need an icon visually, but not as a separate announcement.

tooltip.title: Label-driven tooltip
tooltip.description: When only a label is provided, the icon uses that same value for accessibility and the tooltip hint.
tooltip.label: Search

custom.title: Custom tooltip copy
custom.description: Use a separate tooltip message when the accessible label should stay short but the visible hint needs more context.
custom.label: Preview

integrationCard.title: Composed with buttons
integrationCard.description: The icon primitive also works inside higher-level controls. In those cases, the parent control still owns the accessible name and tooltip behavior.

section.title: Section
section.description: Sections group related content in normal page flow. Use them to define page structure, introduce a title and supporting description, and optionally attach section-level actions.

items.structure: Use sections to organize page-level content and related blocks, not to add visual decoration by default.
items.actions: Section actions belong in the header when they affect the content of that section as a whole.
items.surface: A section does not require borders, shadows, or depth. Add those treatments only when the surrounding layout needs more separation.
items.composition: Sections often contain cards, lists, forms, and other primitives. They define structure, while inner components define density and behavior.

patternNotes.snippet:
Section title Supporting description.
...

structure.title: Structure
structure.description: A section establishes hierarchy and grouping without forcing a boxed surface.

actions.title: Header actions
actions.description: Actions can live in the header when they operate on the section content as a whole.

surface.title: Surface treatment
surface.description: Sections may remain plain or receive additional visual treatment depending on the surrounding layout.

basic.title: Default structure
basic.description: A section usually combines a heading, a short description, and a body region for related content.
basic.body: This is the standard page-level grouping primitive. Use it to introduce content before placing denser blocks such as cards, tables, or forms.

minimal.title: Minimal section
minimal.description: The structure stays useful even when the section body is small and the visual treatment is light.
minimal.body: Not every section needs heavy separation. Sometimes spacing and hierarchy are enough to keep the page understandable.

actionsCard.title: Section-level actions
actionsCard.description: Header actions should affect the section as a whole, not one individual child element inside it.

one.title: Active records
one.description: A child block that belongs to the section body.

two.title: Archived records
two.description: Another child block managed by the same section controls.

decorated.title: Decorated section
decorated.description: Add surface treatment when the layout needs stronger separation, but keep the same structure primitive underneath.
decorated.body: The structure stays the same. Only the visual treatment changes to support the page composition.

plain.title: Plain section
plain.description: Sections can also remain visually quiet and let surrounding spacing do the grouping work.
plain.body: Use the plain form when the page already has enough separation from layout, rhythm, or nearby containers.

tooltip.title: Tooltip
tooltip.description: Tooltips provide short contextual hints for controls and inline UI elements. Use the wrapper directly around custom triggers or rely on automatic tooltips for icon-only buttons.

items.clarify: Use tooltips to clarify compact controls, especially icon-only actions that need visible context on hover or focus.
items.reinforce: Tooltips should reinforce an existing control, not carry essential information by themselves.
items.compact: Keep tooltip copy short and neutral. If the content needs instructions or multiple actions, use another pattern such as helper text, a popover, or a dialog.
items.iconButtons: For icon buttons, the button component already resolves tooltip content from `tooltipContent`, `title`, or the accessible label.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Tooltip content="Open details" side="top" align="center">
  <button type="button">Trigger</button>
</Tooltip>

<Button
  size="icon"
  variant="ghost"
  title="Add item"
  leadingIcon={<Plus />}
/>

automatic.title: Automatic icon tooltip
automatic.description: Icon buttons resolve tooltip content automatically from the props already used for accessibility.

placement.title: Placement
placement.description: Choose the placement that keeps the hint readable near surrounding layout constraints.

richContent.title: Rich content
richContent.description: Tooltip content may include small amounts of structure, but it should remain compact and scannable.

triggerGuidance.title: Trigger guidance
triggerGuidance.description: Use tooltips as supporting guidance, not as the primary source of meaning.

top.name: Top
top.description: Default placement for compact controls and icon actions.

right.name: Right
right.description: Useful beside dense left-aligned navigation and stacked lists.

bottom.name: Bottom
bottom.description: Safer when there is no room above the trigger.

left.name: Left
left.description: Works well near right-edge controls and floating actions.

inlineHint.title: Inline hint
inlineHint.description: Wrap custom inline triggers when you need context without changing the surrounding layout.
inlineHint.prefix: Status updates are available in the
inlineHint.tooltip: Opens the activity stream for this workspace.

compactMetadata.title: Compact metadata
compactMetadata.description: A small amount of structured content is fine when the hint needs a title and one line of context.
compactMetadata.tooltipBody: Reference docs are kept separate from the current flow.

avoidLongInstructions.title: Avoid long instructions
avoidLongInstructions.description: If the message starts reading like onboarding copy, use helper text, a popover, or a dialog instead.

badge.title: Badge
badge.description: Badges communicate compact status, category, or lifecycle metadata without taking over the layout. Use them when a short label should stay visually distinct inside cards, lists, and detail headers.

items.status: Use badge for short status-like metadata such as active state, review stage, or risk level. It should stay compact and glanceable.
items.tone: Choose tone based on meaning, not decoration. The color should reinforce semantic state without carrying the entire message alone.
items.variant: Use softer variants for dense surfaces and stronger variants only when the badge needs a little more emphasis.
items.compact: Keep badge copy short. If the content needs a sentence, helper text, or multiple actions, it is no longer a badge.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Badge tone="success">Approved</Badge>
<Badge tone="brand" variant="outline">Featured</Badge>

tones.title: Semantic tones
tones.description: Badges are most useful when they reinforce state or lifecycle meaning with compact, consistent visual treatment.

variants.title: Variants
variants.description: Use variants to tune emphasis without changing the underlying semantic meaning.

brand.title: Brand highlight
brand.description: Useful for featured entities, promoted filters, or product-owned categories.
brand.label: Featured

success.title: Healthy state
success.description: A compact positive status inside cards, rows, and detail views.
success.label: Approved

warning.title: Needs attention
warning.description: A cautionary state that should remain visible without becoming a destructive alert.
warning.label: Review soon

danger.title: Risk signal
danger.description: Use danger badges sparingly for blocked, failed, or high-risk states.
danger.label: Blocked

breadcrumb.title: Breadcrumb
breadcrumb.description: Breadcrumbs communicate the current location inside nested navigation. Use them when users need quick orientation and a lightweight path back to broader parent areas.

items.location: Use breadcrumbs to orient users inside deeper navigation hierarchies, especially when several parent levels matter to the current page.
items.depth: Keep the trail short and meaningful. If the path becomes noisy or repetitive, the information architecture probably needs adjustment.
items.current: The current page should be visually distinct and should not behave like a navigable link.
items.mobile: Breadcrumbs are helpful context, but they should stay lightweight. On constrained layouts, avoid turning them into dense secondary navigation.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Breadcrumb>
  <BreadcrumbList>
    <BreadcrumbItem>
      <BreadcrumbLink href="#">Projects</BreadcrumbLink>
      <BreadcrumbSeparator />
    </BreadcrumbItem>
    <BreadcrumbItem>
      <BreadcrumbCurrent>Project Atlas</BreadcrumbCurrent>
    </BreadcrumbItem>
  </BreadcrumbList>
</Breadcrumb>

examples.title: Navigation context
examples.description: Breadcrumbs work best when they reinforce page location instead of duplicating a primary nav menu.

standard.title: Standard page trail
standard.description: A simple path for pages that sit one or two levels below a broader parent area.

dense.title: Deeper admin path
dense.description: Use a slightly denser trail when the user may need to step back through a few related parent views.

combobox.title: Combobox
combobox.description: Combobox combines a trigger field with search and option picking. Use it when users must choose one value from a defined set but the list is long enough that search dramatically improves scanning.

items.search: Use combobox when search or filtering is part of the interaction. If the list is short and obvious, a normal select is usually enough.
items.single: This primitive is best for one chosen value at a time. If users need many selections, prefer a dedicated multi-select pattern.
items.metadata: Combobox options can carry brief supporting metadata, but the option list should still remain fast to scan.
items.scope: Use it for people, places, categories, and other datasets where the user benefits from narrowing the list before choosing.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Combobox
  value={value}
  onValueChange={setValue}
  placeholder="Choose owner"
  searchPlaceholder="Search owners"
  options={options}
/>

examples.title: Searchable selection
examples.description: Combobox is useful when the selected value should still look like a field, but the underlying option set needs quick filtering.

people.title: Owner assignment
people.description: A common admin pattern where people are selected from a growing list of options.
people.label: Owner

city.title: City search
city.description: Another good fit when locations or categories become too large for a plain select.
city.label: City

drawer.title: Drawer
drawer.description: Drawers reveal focused side panels while keeping the current page visible underneath. Use them for edit flows, details, and filter panels that should preserve more context than a centered dialog.

items.context: Use a drawer when the current page should remain visible and recognizable while the user handles a focused secondary task.
items.side: Choose the side based on layout and task. Right-side detail panels are common, while left-side drawers often work well for filters and supporting tools.
items.footer: Keep footer actions concise and predictable. A drawer should still feel scoped, not like a full page hidden inside a panel.
items.escalation: If the content becomes highly interruptive or needs stronger focus isolation, escalate to a dialog or a dedicated route instead.

patternNotes.snippet:
<Drawer open={open} onOpenChange={setOpen}>
  <DrawerContent side="right">
    <DrawerHeader>
      <DrawerTitle>Project details</DrawerTitle>
      <DrawerDescription>Review metadata.</DrawerDescription>
    </DrawerHeader>
    <DrawerFooter>...</DrawerFooter>
  </DrawerContent>
</Drawer>

examples.title: Panel patterns
examples.description: Drawers are strongest when the page context should stay visible while the user reviews or edits adjacent information.

details.title: Detail panel
details.description: A right-side drawer works well for record details and light edit flows.

filters.title: Filters panel
filters.description: A left-side drawer can keep supporting filters available without replacing the whole page.

details.title: Project Atlas
details.description: Use the drawer for supporting record detail while the original list or page remains visible underneath.

owner.title: Owner
owner.description: Maria Souza coordinates approvals and delivery checkpoints.

status.title: Status
status.description: Currently active with one pending review cycle.

updated.title: Last update
updated.description: Edited two hours ago by the operations team.

filters.title: Refine project list
filters.description: A supporting drawer can hold denser filters without breaking the surrounding browsing context.

owner.title: Owner
owner.description: Filter by responsible team or reviewer.

period.title: Period
period.description: Limit the result set to a time window.

hero.title: Sign in to keep academic operations controlled and consistent.
hero.description: Use your administrator account to access the application shell, review operational areas, and manage the platform with the shared system surface.

adminOnly.title: Administrator-only access
adminOnly.description: The login entry point is reserved for the admin workspace and protected routes.

session.title: Session continuity
session.description: Secure cookies keep the session stable across the protected application area.

refresh.title: Automatic token refresh
refresh.description: Expired access tokens are refreshed through the middleware before the shell is interrupted.

note.title: Current access model
note.description: The app redirects authenticated users straight to the shell and keeps login as the only public route.

form.title: Login
form.description: Enter your administrator credentials to continue into the PUG operations workspace.

feedback.error: Unable to sign in right now.

currentAccount.title: Current account
currentAccount.description: This section is the first shared server-state example. It reads the authenticated account from the API through TanStack Query instead of page-local fetch state.

error.title: Unable to load the current account.

account.label: Account
account.logoutError: Unable to sign out right now.
